text.tspan: MCU-Friendly Mamba: Optimizing Mamba Model Inference on Microcontrollers with

tspan.tspan: TinyML is a subset of Machine Learning focused on developing models
small enough to run on low-power microcontrollers. By enabling tasks
like Human Activity Recognition, fingerprint detection, and keyword
spotting directly on-device, TinyML facilitates decentralized systems
that operate without a central server. Historically, these applications
have relied on simpler architectures such as MLPs, CNNs, and recurrent
networks like LSTMs or GRUs [1].
Recently, the Mamba [2] architecture has emerged as a compelling
alternative, offering performance that rivals or exceeds Transformers
while maintaining linear scaling relative to context length. This efficiency
in processing long contexts makes Mamba an ideal candidate for
TinyML. However, deploying it to resource-constrained microcontrollers
remains a challenge. Critical optimization techniques, such as
quantization and Low-Rank Factorization, have yet to be fully explored
or implemented for this specific architecture on edge hardware.
Research Question: How can the current work on Mamba model
inference for microcontrollers be improved?
- What are the current limitations of inference?
- How can quantization be applied to Mamba for MCU?
- Can Low-Rank Factorization be used to improve the memory usage?

text.tspan: The Mamba model has been optimized for deployment on modern
GPUs to showcase its competitiveness against Transformer models.
This is a significant challenge when trying to run inference on an MCU.
My choice of microcontroller is ESP32, because of its high
performance and because it was chosen by the authors of MambaLite-
Micro, which will allow for comparisons. The different models of this
chip, however, showcase the impact of the choice of operations that
the manufacturer decides to implement for the microcontroller.
There exist many different ways for
ExecuTorch, or a raw C implementation.
We decided to use LiteRT, as it is

does not give as much freedom in as
a raw C implementation, it is much easier to introduce modifications
The Mamba architecture features a dual-path design consisting of a
gated path and a selective State Space Model (SSM) path. At its core,
the model processes inputs sequentially through a hidden internal
state, but it departs from traditional structured SSMs by making its
recurrence parameters, specifically the discretization step Delta and
the transition matrices B and C, direct functions of the current input
token rather than static constants. By making these parameters input-
information to compress or filter out based
on context. Furthermore, to overcome the
on the GPU, caching intermediate states in
fast SRAM instead of slower HBM to achieve

tspan.tspan: The memory on the other hand
has been reduced, when using
INT8 model. The reduction however
is far from the ideal 4x (it is around
50%). This is most probably caused
by the additional quantization and
dequantization operations.
The Mamba architecture shows promise for highly efficient TinyML models.
It's SSM operation could allow for improving the length of context that
microcontrollers can process. However, this operation is not supported by
any of the TinyML frameworks ExecuTorch, LiteRT, ONNX. Defining the
SSM as a custom operation and enabling it to be quantized would help
eliminate the amount of operations done on small inputs, which could
improve performance.
As shown in the results, Mamba is strongly dependent on exponential
function, which is slow to compute, so it might be worth redesigning it
around the hardware constraints introduced by microcontrollers.
[1] M. Tri Lê, P. Wolinski, and J. Arbel, “Efficient Neural Networks for Tiny Machine
Learning: A
Comprehensive Review,” ACM Trans. Intell. Syst. Technol., vol. 17, no. 4,
[2] A. Gu and T. Dao, “Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State
[3] G. Franco, A. Pappalardo, and N. J. Fraser, "Xilinx/brevitas," Zenodo, 2025. doi:
I tested performance of a Mamba model using the Human Activity
Recognition dataset. It contains data collected and preprocessed from an
IMU (combination of accelerometer and gyroscope data) about people
performing one of 6 actions (classes) - walking, walking upstairs, walking
downstairs, sitting, standing and laying.
I trained the model using architecture used in the previous research (fully
connected, mamba, pooling, fully connected layers). The training was
performed with PyTorch and achieved accuracy of 94.3% (98.5% on
validation set). Then the model got converted to LiteRT, without any
accuracy loss.
The model then got quantized to INT8 (both weights and activations),
using the Ai Edge Quantizer. This reduced the accuracy to 94.0% (0.3%
decrease). Finally the model was converted to a C array and put into an
ESP32 project using ESP TFLite Micro.
Inference was run on a ESP32 on a random sample from the dataset. The
memory usage was measured using the TFLite's allocator's output.
Latency was measured per operation type in order to find the bottlenecks
in the system.
Latency analisys of the inference shows vastly different results for the
float and INT8 quantized models. While the integer operations are
faster, performing them on quantized tensors involves some
overhead, which the results show outweights the gains from INT8.
This caused the inference of the quantized model to be significantly
slower (205 ms vs 85 ms). This is also partially caused by the ESP32
used not having any hardware acceleration for INT8 models.
